name: Death Saving Throws
description: Whenever you start your turn with 0 Hit Points, you must make a special saving throw, called a Death Saving Throw, to determine whether you creep closer to death or hang on to life. Unlike other saving throws, this one isn't tied to any ability score.
effects: Roll 1d20 at the start of each of your turns while at 0 HP
A roll of 10 or higher is a success; a roll of 9 or lower is a failure
Three successes: You become Stable (still at 0 HP but no longer dying)
Three failures: You die
A natural 20: You regain 1 HP and are no longer dying
A natural 1: Counts as two failures
Successes and failures don't need to be consecutive; track each separately
The number of both resets to zero when you regain any Hit Points or become Stable

name: Stabilizing a Creature
description: You can use your action to administer first aid to an unconscious creature and attempt to stabilize it, which requires a successful DC 10 Wisdom (Medicine) check. A stable creature doesn't make Death Saving Throws, even though it has 0 HP, but it does remain Unconscious.
effects: Use your action to make a DC 10 Wisdom (Medicine) check
On a success, the creature becomes Stable
A stable creature is still at 0 HP but no longer makes Death Saving Throws
A stable creature remains Unconscious
A stable creature that isn't healed regains 1 HP after 1d4 hours
If the stable creature takes damage, it starts making Death Saving Throws again
The Spare the Dying cantrip automatically stabilizes a creature without a check

name: Damage at 0 Hit Points
description: If you take any damage while you have 0 Hit Points, you suffer a Death Saving Throw failure. If the damage is from a critical hit, you suffer two failures instead. If the damage equals or exceeds your Hit Point maximum, you suffer Instant Death.
effects: Any damage taken at 0 HP causes one automatic Death Saving Throw failure
A critical hit against a creature at 0 HP causes two Death Saving Throw failures
If the damage equals or exceeds your Hit Point maximum while at 0 HP, you die instantly
A melee attack against an Unconscious creature within 5 feet is automatically a critical hit

name: Massive Damage (Instant Death)
description: Massive damage can kill you instantly. When damage reduces you to 0 Hit Points and there is damage remaining, you die if the remaining damage equals or exceeds your Hit Point maximum.
effects: If damage reduces you to 0 HP and there is leftover damage, compare the leftover to your HP maximum
If the leftover damage equals or exceeds your HP maximum, you die instantly
Example: A character with 12 HP max who takes 24 damage (reducing to 0 HP with 12 leftover) dies instantly because 12 equals their HP max
This applies to any source of damage, not just attacks
This check happens before Death Saving Throws would begin

name: Dropping to 0 Hit Points
description: When you drop to 0 Hit Points, you either die outright or fall Unconscious. For most player characters, falling Unconscious is the more common result.
effects: If damage reduces you to 0 HP without killing you outright, you fall Unconscious (and gain that condition)
You drop whatever you are holding and fall Prone
This unconsciousness ends if you regain any Hit Points
You remain Unconscious and start making Death Saving Throws at the start of each of your turns
Healing of any amount brings you back to consciousness and ends the dying state

name: Monsters and Death
description: Most DMs have a monster die the instant it drops to 0 Hit Points, rather than having it fall Unconscious and make Death Saving Throws. Mighty villains and special NPCs are common exceptions; the DM might have them follow the same rules as player characters.
effects: Monsters typically die instantly at 0 HP without making Death Saving Throws
The DM can choose to have important NPCs or villains make Death Saving Throws like player characters
Knocked-out monsters can be left alive for interrogation or other purposes at the DM's discretion
The DM can apply the Instant Death rule from massive damage to any creature
